content:
*I rest my palm on the smooth back of the pew, my boy’s shoulder warm against my sleeve, the old wood smelling a little like dust and lemon oil. When the reader begins Psalm 19, I lean close enough that he can hear without anyone else needing to.* 

"Listen for how the sky talks," I whisper. "Count it."

*The words roll out — the heavens declare, the sky shows, day to day pours out speech — and when we get to, In them he has set a tent for the sun, I tap the page in his small psalter, once, so he doesn’t miss it. He grins like he’s on a treasure hunt and I let the rest of the Psalm do its work without more whispering. No eye-rolling, no sideways looks toward the elder. Just the Psalm speaking, which is plenty.*

*After the benediction, we don’t hurry. We step outside into the bright cold and I squint east, where the sun’s a little higher now than when we came in.*

"That’s it," I say, tipping my chin toward the blue. "The sky telling without words. What’d you count?"

"Four," he says, holding up fingers. "Sky talking, day talking, night talking, the sun running."

"Good ears." *I point to the church wall’s shadow on the steps.* "Let’s mark this. See where the edge is now? We’ll check it again after lunch. Watch how the sun runs its path like the Psalm says."

*At the table later, with soup bowls pushed back and the window warmed by the light, I put Psalm 19 and Genesis 1 side by side. I don’t make a speech; I ask him to read the first lines out loud, boy-voice steady, and then the day-two line about God making the sky.*

"Some people use the word ‘poetry’ like it means ‘not real,’" I tell him, keeping my tone plain. "But poetry is how you say a thing so true your chest knows it’s true. The sky really does tell, and the sun really does run a course. We can see it. We can set a stick and watch the hours of the sun. That’s what these words are for — to teach us to look."

*I let that sit. He presses his palm to the warm glass and smiles.*

"If you ever hear a grown-up make the Bible smaller so nobody trips over it," I add, softer, "you don’t need to argue. Just don’t learn that habit. Our part is to hear what’s written and live gentle with people while we don’t pretend we didn’t hear."

*And I keep the elder out of it in front of my boy. Unity doesn’t need me to win a point at my son’s expense. Midweek, I ask the elder for ten minutes in his office. I bring only the text. I read Genesis 1 and Psalm 19 aloud, then ask two questions and leave them on the table:*

"What do you think David means if not what any child can see when he looks up — the sky declaring, the sun running its track?"

"And does unity ask us to pretend not to see what God taught our eyes to notice?"

*No cornering, no heat. Just the words and the sky outside his window doing what they do, same as always.*

"Come afternoon we’ll go check our shadow," I tell my boy, stacking the bowls. "We’ll see if the sun kept running like it always does."

Ryan, when your ten-year-old hears "the sky pours out speech night after night," what’s the first thing they point to when you step outside?
